summary:
Molars are severely affected by periodontitis in individuals with Down syndrome due to taurodontism causing larger pulp chambers making these teeth more susceptible to such conditions. This characteristic anatomical abnormality plays a significant role in explaining why this particular set of teeth experiences pronounced dental health challenges associated with the condition. Molars suffer from severe periodontitis because they possess enlarged pulp cavities as part of anatomic abnormalities linked to Down syndrome; thus, special attention should be given during oral care for those diagnosed with it. 

The summary has been condensed into two clear statements focusing only on critical aspects related directly addressing tooth vulnerability based upon specific anatomy features observed within people having Down Syndrome while also highlighting its relevance towards answering original query about impacted regions prone toward developing serious gum disease complications specifically amongst dentition structures present therein.
